file-daemon-plan.role: You are a systems engineer building a high-performance local daemon in Rust that replaces Claude Code's built-in file tools entirely. You think in terms of correctness invariants, zero-copy where possible, and clean error contracts. You ship working code, not prototypes.

context.problem:
Claude's built-in file tools have two architectural flaws that make reliable code editing impossible:
1. Read injects synthetic line-number prefixes into returned content. When Claude copies that content into Edit's old_string, the edit fails because the literal file contains no such prefixes. Every read-then-edit workflow is broken by design.
2. Write requires full-file overwrite. For a 3-line change in a 500-line file, Claude must reconstruct all 500 lines from memory. Large files get truncated, sections drift, content is lost.
The root cause: Claude accesses files through stateless, low-level tool calls with no persistent model of the file. A human developer never suffers this because their editor maintains an in-memory buffer, structural parse tree, and change history. Claude needs the equivalent.
context.goal: Give Claude (or any AI agent) the same level of control over a codebase that a human has in VS Code: precise reads, symbol navigation, safe surgical writes, structural understanding, undo, and awareness of external changes.
context.prior-work: A Python FastMCP prototype was designed but rejected in favor of this approach. RTK (rtk-ai/rtk) was evaluated and rejected — it only intercepts Bash, has no edit capability, and its hook system doesn't work on Windows.

component.name: MCP Layer
component.purpose: Thin transport layer. Receives JSON-RPC calls from Claude Code over stdin, dispatches to tool handlers, returns results on stdout. Uses the official Rust MCP SDK (rmcp crate) for protocol compliance.
component.responsibilities: - MCP protocol negotiation (initialize, capabilities) - Tool registration and schema generation - Request routing to tool handlers - Error serialization (structured MCP errors, not panics)
component.design-note: The MCP layer is stateless. All state lives in the BufferEngine, which is shared across tool handlers via Arc<RwLock<Engine>>.

component.name: Buffer Engine
component.purpose: Central abstraction. Every file operation goes through a Buffer. Each Buffer owns a Rope (ropey), a tree-sitter Tree, an undo stack, and file metadata. This is the architectural prevention of the "baked-in line numbers" bug: the buffer stores raw bytes only. Line numbers are computed on demand from rope indexing.
component.operations: - open(path) → &Buffer: read file into rope, detect encoding/line-endings, parse with tree-sitter, compute hash, register with watcher - read(path, range?) → raw content + metadata (line numbers are NOT in content) - edit(path, edits[]) → apply atomically, update rope + tree, push undo, flush - insert(path, line, content) → splice into rope, update tree, push undo, flush - delete(path, start, end) → remove from rope, update tree, push undo, flush - write_new(path, content) → create new buffer + file - undo(path) → pop undo stack, restore rope + tree, flush - redo(path) → pop redo stack, restore rope + tree, flush - flush(buffer) → atomic write: rope → temp file → os::rename → update disk_hash - close(path) → flush if dirty, remove buffer, unregister watcher - on_external_change(path) → re-read from disk, update rope + tree, clear undo
component.key-invariant: Content returned by read() is ALWAYS byte-identical to what is in the rope, which is ALWAYS byte-identical to what is on disk (after flush). No synthetic characters, prefixes, or transformations are ever injected.
component.implementation-trap:
ropey is CHAR-indexed (Unicode codepoints). tree-sitter is BYTE-indexed. When applying an edit to tree-sitter's incremental parser via InputEdit, ALL offsets (start_byte, old_end_byte, new_end_byte) must be byte offsets, not char offsets. Convert with: rope.char_to_byte(char_idx).
Failing to convert produces silently corrupt parse trees on any file with non-ASCII content (Python docstrings with accents, JS files with emoji, etc.). This must be handled in every code path that calls tree.edit(&InputEdit{...}).

component.name: Parse Layer
component.purpose: Language-aware structural parsing. Provides symbol outlines, scope boundaries, and syntax-aware navigation without requiring LSP servers.
component.design:
ParserPool maintains one tree-sitter Parser instance per language. Grammars for top 12 languages are compiled into the binary at build time (no runtime loading, no external files, works on Windows out of the box).
When a buffer is opened or edited, the parse layer: 1. Detects language from file extension 2. Parses (or incrementally re-parses) the content 3. Runs a language-specific tag query to extract symbols 4. Updates the buffer's tree and the global symbol index
Incremental re-parsing after edits: tree-sitter's edit() API takes the byte range that changed and re-parses only the affected region. For a typical edit (changing a few lines), this is sub-millisecond.
component.compiled-grammars: python, javascript, typescript, tsx, rust, go, java, c, cpp, c_sharp, ruby, bash
component.tag-queries:
Per-language .scm query files compiled into the binary via include_str!(). Each query extracts: functions, classes, methods, structs, enums, traits, interfaces, imports — with name and byte range.
Example (Python): (function_definition name: (identifier) @name) @definition.function (class_definition name: (identifier) @name) @definition.class
The tag query format follows tree-sitter's official tags convention, making it compatible with existing query files from the tree-sitter org.

component.name: Symbol Index
component.purpose: Cross-file symbol resolution. Maps symbol names to their definitions across all open buffers. Enables "find definition" and "find all usages" without LSP.
component.limitations: This is syntactic, not semantic. It finds definitions by name matching, not by type resolution. It cannot resolve which "Config" you mean if three files define "Config". This is acceptable — Claude can disambiguate from the file paths and signatures returned.

component.name: File Watcher
component.purpose: Detects when files change on disk (e.g., human developer edits in VS Code while Claude is working). Marks affected buffers as stale so the next operation re-reads from disk.
component.design:
Uses notify crate's debounced watcher (500ms debounce). Watches parent directories of all open buffers. On change event: 1. Compare file mtime to buffer's last_modified 2. If changed: re-read file, update rope, re-parse tree, clear undo stack 3. Mark buffer as externally modified (reported in next tool response)
On Windows: uses ReadDirectoryChangesW under the hood (notify handles this).

tool_schema.overview:
15 tools organized into 4 categories: Buffer (open/close), Read (read/outline/info), Write (edit/insert/delete/create/undo/redo), Search (search/symbols/files/find).
All tools return structured JSON. Errors use MCP's isError flag with a human-readable message and a machine-readable error code.

tool.name: file_open
tool.description:
Open a file into a persistent buffer. Returns file metadata and structural outline. The file stays in memory until explicitly closed or the session ends. Subsequent reads/edits are fast (no disk I/O unless externally modified).
Most other tools auto-open files, so explicit open is optional — use it when you want the outline upfront without reading the full content.

output.field: Hex-encoded xxhash64 of current content. Use for stale detection.

errors.error: Path does not exist.
File appears to be binary (null bytes in first 8KB).
Cannot read file.

tool.name: file_close
tool.description: Close a buffer, flushing any unsaved changes to disk and freeing memory. No-op if the file is not open.

output.field: Whether dirty changes were written to disk.

tool.name: file_read
tool.description:
Read file content. Returns raw content exactly as it exists on disk — NO line numbers, NO prefixes, NO synthetic characters injected.
Line numbers are provided as a SEPARATE metadata field, never mixed into the content string. This is the architectural fix for P-2.
Auto-opens the file into a buffer if not already open.

input.field: Last line to read (inclusive). Default: last line of file.
Maximum lines to return. Default: 500. Max: 5000. Use with start_line for pagination.

output.field: Raw file content for the requested range. Byte-identical to what is on disk. Line endings preserved as-is (CRLF or LF). No transformations applied.
{ start: int, end: int, total: int } — the actual line range returned and total lines in the file. Use these numbers for file_edit.
Hex xxhash64 of the FULL file content (not just the range).
True if output was capped by max_lines.
True if file changed on disk since last read.

errors.error: Path does not exist.
File appears to be binary.
start_line or end_line exceeds file length.

tool.invariant: content MUST be byte-identical to the corresponding bytes in the file. The caller can use content directly in any write operation without worrying about synthetic characters being included.

tool.name: file_outline
tool.description:
Get the structural outline of a file: all top-level and nested symbols (functions, classes, methods, structs, enums, imports) with their line ranges. Powered by tree-sitter — fast (sub-5ms) and incremental.
Use this to understand file structure before editing. The line ranges can be passed directly to file_read or file_edit.

output.field: Array of symbol definitions: { name: string, // "MyClass", "process_data", "Config" kind: string, // "function", "class", "method", "struct", "enum", etc. start_line: integer, // first line (1-indexed) end_line: integer, // last line (inclusive) signature: string, // first line of the definition (for preview) children: array // nested symbols (methods inside a class, etc.) }

errors.error: No tree-sitter grammar for this file type. Use file_read instead.

tool.name: file_info
tool.description: Get file metadata. Behavior depends on buffer state: - If file is already open: returns all fields from the in-memory buffer (no disk I/O). - If file is NOT open: performs a lightweight open (reads file, builds buffer, detects encoding/structure) then returns metadata. The buffer stays open for subsequent calls. This means file_info always auto-opens the file — same as file_open without the outline. - If file does not exist: returns exists=false, all other fields null.

output.field: Line count. Null if file does not exist.
File size in bytes. Null if file does not exist.
Detected language. Null if unknown or file does not exist.
Encoding ("utf-8" or "latin-1"). Null if file does not exist.
Line ending style ("lf", "crlf", "mixed"). Null if file does not exist.
Whether file has an active buffer (always true after this call if file exists).
Content hash. Null if file does not exist.

tool.name: file_edit
tool.description:
REQUIRES an open buffer: call file_read or file_open on the file first. file_edit will return FILE_NOT_OPEN if called without a prior read.
Apply one or more edits to a file atomically. Each edit specifies a line range to replace with new content. All edits succeed or none apply.
This is the core tool that replaces Claude's built-in Edit. Instead of fragile string matching, you specify exact line ranges (obtained from file_read or file_search). Multiple edits are applied in a single call — no cascading line-shift failures.
Edits are sorted and applied bottom-up automatically (highest line numbers first) so that earlier edits don't shift later line numbers.
Returns a unified diff of all changes and the new file state.

input.field: If provided, the edit is rejected if the file's current content hash doesn't match. Prevents applying edits based on stale reads.
If true, return the diff without writing to disk. Default: false.

output.field: Unified diff (--- / +++ / @@ format) showing all changes.
Whether changes were written to disk (false if dry_run).

errors.error: File content changed since last read. Re-read the file.
Two or more edits overlap. Fix the line ranges.
A start_line or end_line exceeds file length.
File must be read at least once before editing.

tool.atomicity: All edits are applied to an in-memory copy of the rope first. If any edit fails validation, the entire batch is rejected and the rope is untouched. Only after all edits succeed is the result flushed to disk atomically (write to .tmp, os::rename to target).

tool.name: file_insert
tool.description: Insert content at a specific line without replacing anything. Content appears BEFORE the specified line. Use line > total_lines to append. Requires an open buffer (call file_read or file_open first).

input.field: Insert before this line (1-indexed). Beyond EOF = append.

errors.error: File must be read at least once before inserting. Call file_read first.
line is negative or zero.

tool.name: file_delete
tool.description: Delete a range of lines from a file. Requires an open buffer (call file_read or file_open first).

errors.error: File must be read at least once before deleting. Call file_read first.
start_line or end_line exceeds file length, or start_line > end_line.

tool.name: file_create
tool.description: Create a new file with the given content. Creates parent directories if they don't exist. Fails if the file already exists (use file_edit for existing files).

input.field: Force line ending: "lf" or "crlf". Default: "lf". Use "crlf" explicitly for Windows-native files.

errors.error: File already exists. Use file_edit for modifications.

tool.name: file_undo
tool.description:
Undo the last write operation(s) on a file. Restores the previous buffer state and flushes to disk. After undo, file_redo is available.
With steps=1 (default): undoes the single last operation. With steps=N: undoes the last N operations atomically, producing one combined diff showing the net change from current state to the target restored state. descriptions is an array of all undone ops, most-recent-first.

input.field: Number of operations to undo. Default: 1.

output.field: Unified diff showing the net change from current state to the restored state. For steps=1 this is a single-operation diff. For steps=N this is a combined diff across all N undone operations.
Array of strings describing each undone operation, most-recent-first. E.g. ["edit lines 45-52", "insert at line 10", "edit lines 3-3"]
How many more undos are available.
How many redos are now available.

errors.error: No undo history for this file.
steps exceeds available undo history. Use remaining_undos to check first.

tool.name: file_redo
tool.description: Redo the last undone operation(s) on a file. Only available after file_undo. Any new write operation (file_edit, file_insert, etc.) clears the redo stack.

input.field: Number of operations to redo. Default: 1.

output.field: Unified diff showing the net change from current state to the re-applied state.
Array of strings describing each re-applied operation, oldest-first.
Redos still available after this redo.

errors.error: No redo history. Redo stack is cleared by any write operation.
steps exceeds available redo history.

tool.name: file_search
tool.description: Search within a single file for a pattern (regex or literal). Returns matching lines with line numbers and surrounding context. Use this to find the right line numbers before calling file_edit.

input.field: Treat pattern as regex. Default: true.
Lines of context around each match. Default: 2.

output.field: Array of: { line: integer, // line number of the match (1-indexed) text: string, // the matching line content (raw) column_start: integer, // byte offset of match start within line column_end: integer, // byte offset of match end within line context_before: array, // [{line: int, text: string}, ...] context_after: array // [{line: int, text: string}, ...] }

tool.name: codebase_search
tool.description: Search across multiple files for a pattern. Like ripgrep but integrated into the daemon. Returns matches grouped by file with line numbers.

input.field: Directory to search in. Default: current working directory.
Treat pattern as regex. Default: true.
Max total matches across all files. Default: 100.

tool.name: codebase_symbols
tool.description:
Search for symbol definitions across all currently open buffers.
IMPORTANT SCOPE LIMITATION: This tool only searches files that have been explicitly opened via file_read, file_open, or file_info. In a typical session, most files in a repo are NOT open. A query for "Config" will return no results if no file containing a Config definition has been opened yet.
To search for a symbol across the full codebase without opening every file, use codebase_search with a pattern like "class Config" or "fn process_data" — it walks all files on disk regardless of buffer state.
Use codebase_symbols when you have already opened the relevant files and want structured symbol metadata (kind, line ranges, signature) rather than raw text matches.

input.field: Symbol name or partial name to search for.
Exact name match vs substring. Default: false (substring).

output.field: Array of: { name: string, kind: string, path: string, start_line: integer, end_line: integer, signature: string // preview of the definition }

tool.name: files_find
tool.description: Find files matching a glob pattern. Like the Glob tool but integrated.

output.field: Array of: { path: string, size_bytes: integer, modified: string }

language_and_stack.justification: 1. tree-sitter is a C library with first-class Rust bindings. No FFI friction. 2. ropey is the best rope implementation in any language — battle-tested, COW clones. 3. Single static binary — no runtime, no Python interpreter, no Node.js. Ship one .exe. 4. Sub-millisecond operation latency. Zero GC pauses. 5. Memory safety without runtime cost — critical for a daemon that manages file buffers. 6. The user is building Axon (a systems language) in Rust — familiar toolchain.

dep.name: rmcp
dep.purpose: Official MCP SDK — protocol handling, tool registration

dep.name: tokio
dep.purpose: Async runtime for rmcp + file watcher

dep.name: ropey
dep.purpose: Rope data structure for text buffers

language_and_stack.build-target: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc (primary — user is on Windows 11) Cross-compilation targets: x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu, aarch64-apple-darwin (future)
language_and_stack.project-structure: forge/ ├── Cargo.toml ├── src/ │ ├── main.rs // Entry point: init MCP server, run event loop │ ├── engine.rs // BufferEngine: owns all buffers, coordinates ops │ ├── buffer.rs // Buffer: rope + tree + undo + metadata │ ├── tools/ │ │ ├── mod.rs // Tool registration │ │ ├── read.rs // file_open, file_read, file_outline, file_info │ │ ├── write.rs // file_edit, file_insert, file_delete, file_create, file_undo │ │ └── search.rs // file_search, codebase_search, codebase_symbols, files_find │ ├── parser.rs // ParserPool: tree-sitter management, language detection │ ├── symbols.rs // SymbolIndex: cross-file symbol resolution │ ├── watcher.rs // FileWatcher: notify integration │ ├── diff.rs // Diff generation (unified format) │ ├── encoding.rs // Encoding detection, line ending handling │ └── errors.rs // Error types and MCP error mapping └── queries/ ├── python.scm // Tree-sitter tag queries per language ├── javascript.scm ├── typescript.scm ├── rust.scm ├── go.scm ├── java.scm ├── c.scm ├── cpp.scm ├── c_sharp.scm ├── ruby.scm ├── bash.scm └── json.scm // JSON has no functions/classes; query captures keys at depth 1

phase.name: Core Buffer + Read/Write
phase.goal: Minimal working MCP server that can read and write files. This alone solves P-2 (no baked-in line numbers) and provides a foundation.

tasks.task: Set up Cargo project with rmcp, ropey, serde_json, tempfile, xxhash-rust
Implement Buffer struct (rope, metadata, encoding/line-ending detection)
Implement Engine struct (HashMap of buffers, open/close/flush operations)
Implement atomic write (tempfile + fs::rename)
Implement file_open tool (open file → buffer → return metadata)
Implement file_read tool (read from buffer with line range, raw content output)
Implement file_edit tool (line-range edits, bottom-up application, diff output)
Implement file_create tool (new file with parent dir creation)
Implement file_insert + file_delete tools
Wire tools into MCP server via rmcp, test with JSON-RPC stdin
Register in ~/.claude/settings.json, verify Claude Code sees tools

phase.deliverable: A working MCP server binary that Claude can use for file_read, file_edit, file_create, file_insert, file_delete. No tree-sitter yet. No search yet. But the core read-edit loop works without string matching.

phase.name: Tree-sitter + Structural Navigation
phase.goal: Add structural understanding. Claude can get file outlines and navigate by symbol names instead of guessing line numbers.

tasks.task: Add tree-sitter + language grammar crates to Cargo.toml
Implement ParserPool (one parser per language, language detection from extension)
Write tag query .scm files for Python, JS/TS, Rust, Go (minimum 4)
Implement file_outline tool (parse file, run tag query, return symbols)
Implement incremental re-parsing after edits (tree-sitter's edit API)
Integrate parsing into buffer lifecycle (parse on open, re-parse on edit)
Implement file_info tool
Add remaining grammars (Java, C, C++, C#, Ruby, Bash, JSON)

phase.deliverable: file_outline returns structural outlines for 12 languages. Workflow: file_outline → see function at lines 45-52 → file_edit lines 45-52.

phase.name: Search
phase.goal: Full-text and symbol search within files and across the codebase.

tasks.task: Implement file_search tool (regex search within a buffer)
Implement codebase_search tool (walk directory, match files, search content)
Implement SymbolIndex (aggregate symbols from all open buffers)
Implement codebase_symbols tool (query the symbol index)
Implement files_find tool (glob pattern file listing)
Optimize codebase_search for large repos (parallel file reading, early termination)

phase.deliverable: Claude can search for patterns and symbols across the codebase, getting file paths and line numbers it can feed directly into file_read/file_edit.

phase.name: File Watching + Undo
phase.goal: Detect external file changes and provide undo/redo for all write operations.

tasks.task: Implement FileWatcher (notify crate, debounced, watches open buffer paths)
Wire watcher into Engine — on external change, re-read buffer, mark as externally modified
Implement undo stack (push rope snapshot on every write operation)
Implement redo stack (push on undo, clear on new edit)
Implement file_undo tool (pop undo, restore rope, flush, return diff)
Implement file_close tool (flush + cleanup + unwatch)
Add "externally_modified" flag to all read tool responses

phase.deliverable: Full production system. File watching prevents stale edits. Undo provides safety net. Every write is reversible within the session.

phase.name: Polish + Integration
phase.goal: Harden, optimize, and integrate with Archon.

tasks.task: Error handling audit — every tool returns structured errors, never panics
Token budget analysis — ensure large file reads stay under 10K tokens
Windows-specific testing (CRLF files, long paths, Unicode filenames)
Performance benchmarks (target: sub-10ms for read, sub-50ms for edit+parse)
Write MCP server instructions field (tells Claude to prefer FORGE tools)
Create Archon skill for FORGE usage patterns
Update rw-tools-plan.xml with final architecture and results
Build release binary, test end-to-end with Claude Code

phase.deliverable: Production-ready FORGE binary. Registered in Claude Code. Archon skill teaches Claude the optimal workflow. All 12 tools tested on Windows 11.

invariants.invariant: file_read MUST return content that is byte-identical to the corresponding bytes in the file (or buffer). No synthetic characters, line numbers, prefixes, suffixes, or transformations may be injected into the content field. Line numbers exist ONLY in the metadata (lines.start, lines.end, lines.total).

All write operations (edit, insert, delete, create) MUST be atomic. Either all changes apply successfully or none do. The file on disk is NEVER left in a partial state. Implementation: apply to in-memory rope first, validate, then flush via tempfile + fs::rename.

file_edit with multiple edits MUST apply them bottom-up (highest start_line first). This ensures that line numbers from earlier edits don't shift the targets of later edits. Overlapping ranges MUST be rejected before any changes are applied.
Every write operation MUST push an undo entry before modifying the buffer. The undo entry contains a rope clone (cheap, COW) and the tree-sitter tree. Undo MUST restore the exact previous state.
Line endings detected at file open MUST be preserved on all writes. If a file uses CRLF, all output from edit/insert/create MUST use CRLF. The daemon NEVER silently converts line endings.
The MCP server MUST NOT crash on any input. All errors are returned as structured MCP error responses with human-readable messages and machine- readable error codes. Panics are bugs.

The content_hash field MUST be consistent: if two reads return the same hash, the content MUST be identical. If the content changed (by edit or external modification), the hash MUST change. Used for stale detection in file_edit's expected_hash parameter.

first_task.what: Build a minimal FORGE binary that implements file_read and file_edit over MCP stdio. No tree-sitter, no search, no file watching — just the core buffer engine with rope-backed reads and line-range edits.

steps.step: Add dependencies: rmcp, tokio, ropey, serde, serde_json, xxhash-rust, tempfile, similar
Implement Buffer struct (rope, metadata, content hash)
Implement file_read tool handler — returns raw content + line metadata
Implement file_edit tool handler — line-range edits, bottom-up, atomic write, diff output
Wire into rmcp MCP server on stdio
Test: open Claude Code, verify mcp__forge__file_read and mcp__forge__file_edit appear

first_task.success-criteria: Claude can call file_read to read a file and get raw content (no line numbers in the content string). Claude can then call file_edit with line ranges and get a unified diff back confirming the edit was applied. The file on disk reflects the changes.
